title: Newton's Laws of Motion
learningObjectives: State and apply Newton's three laws of motion
Use F = ma to solve dynamics problems
introText: Newton's three laws of motion form the foundation of classical mechanics and describe how forces affect the motion of objects.

steps.heading: Newton's First Law – Inertia
steps.body:
An object will remain at rest or in uniform motion in a straight line unless acted upon by a net external force.

This is also called the law of inertia. Mass is a measure of an object's resistance to changes in motion.
steps.deepDive: Galileo first identified inertia. Newton formalised it as his first law. In the absence of friction, a moving object on a flat surface would continue forever.

steps.heading: Newton's Second Law – F = ma
steps.body:
The net force on an object equals its mass multiplied by its acceleration:

F_net = ma

Force is measured in Newtons (N), mass in kilograms (kg), and acceleration in m/s². If multiple forces act, use vector addition to find the net force.
steps.deepDive: More precisely, F_net = dp/dt (rate of change of momentum). F = ma applies when mass is constant, which is true for most NCEA problems.

steps.heading: Newton's Third Law – Action & Reaction
steps.body:
For every action there is an equal and opposite reaction.

When object A exerts a force on object B, object B exerts an equal and opposite force on object A. These forces always act on different objects and never cancel each other out.
steps.deepDive: Common misconception: action-reaction pairs never act on the same object. The normal force on a book resting on a table is NOT Newton's 3rd pair to gravity – the pair to gravity is the book pulling Earth upward.

steps.questionText: A 5 kg object accelerates at 4 m/s². What is the net force acting on it?

questions.questionText: A 10 kg object has forces of 40 N and 20 N acting in opposite directions. What is its acceleration?
questions.explanation: Net force = 40 – 20 = 20 N. a = F/m = 20/10 = 2 m/s²
